options.content: It predicts the highest intensity sustainable without rapid fatigue

parts.content: Label the parts of a mitochondrion and explain the role of each structure in cellular respiration.
parts.markscheme:
### Labelling mitochondrial structures <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Outer membrane
  - Allows passage of small molecules/ions
  - Controls what enters/leaves mitochondrion
- Inner membrane
  - Site of electron transport chain/oxidative phosphorylation
  - Folded into cristae to increase surface area
- Cristae
  - Infoldings of inner membrane
  - Increase surface area for electron transport chain
- Matrix
  - Location of Krebs cycle enzymes
  - Site of link reaction/Krebs cycle
- Intermembrane space
  - Separates outer and inner membranes

### Roles in cellular respiration

- Outer membrane: Regulates entry/exit of molecules for respiration
- Inner membrane/cristae: Site of electron transport chain/oxidative phosphorylation to produce ATP
- Matrix: Location of enzymes for link reaction and Krebs cycle
- Intermembrane space: Allows movement of hydrogen ions $H^+$ during electron transport

<note>Award marks for correctly labelling structures and linking roles to cellular respiration processes</note>

questions.specification: Which physiological factor limits $VO_{2\text{max}}$ most in elite athletes?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which physiological factor most limits VO₂max in elite athletes, which directly falls under factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max).

options.content: Maximal cardiac output
options.markscheme: Correct. In elite endurance athletes, peripheral adaptations (e.g., mitochondrial capacity) are already highly developed, so $VO_{2\text{max}}$ is most commonly limited by central oxygen delivery. By the Fick equation, $\dot V O_{2\text{max}} = Q_{\text{max}} \times (a-\bar vO_2\,\text{diff})_{\text{max}}$, and $Q_{\text{max}}$ (maximal cardiac output) is typically the primary constraint.

options.content: Mitochondrial enzyme activity
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Mitochondrial enzymes contribute to peripheral oxygen utilization and the $(a-\bar vO_2\,\text{diff})_{\text{max}}$ term, but in elite athletes this is usually not the main limiting factor compared with central oxygen delivery (maximal cardiac output).

options.content: Blood haemoglobin concentration
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Haemoglobin concentration affects arterial oxygen content and thus oxygen transport, but in elite athletes the single most commonly cited limiting factor for $VO_{2\text{max}}$ is maximal cardiac output rather than haemoglobin concentration alone.

options.content: Muscle capillary density
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Capillary density supports peripheral oxygen extraction and can influence $(a-\bar vO_2\,\text{diff})_{\text{max}}$, but in elite athletes peripheral extraction is typically near maximal, so $VO_{2\text{max}}$ is more often limited by maximal cardiac output.

questions.specification:
Researchers analyzed the relative contribution of the three energy systems, phosphagen (ATP-PCr), anaerobic glycolytic, and oxidative, across five Olympic sports. The activities varied in intensity and duration, providing insight into energy system usage along the energy continuum. The data in the table summarizes the estimated percentage contribution of each energy system for each sport based on simulated competition conditions.

| Sport     | ATP-PCr (%) | Glycolytic (%) | Oxidative (%) |
| --------- | ----------- | -------------- | ------------- |
| Karate    | 28          | 30             | 42            |
| Taekwondo | 30          | 4              | 66            |
| Boxing    | 10          | 4              | 86            |
| Wrestling | 23          | 43             | 34            |
| Judo      | 28          | 28             | 44            |

<Note> Any two

parts.content: Describe three functions of water during physical activity.
parts.markscheme:
- Regulates body temperature via sweat production and evaporation from the skin <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains plasma volume/blood volume to support stroke volume and blood pressure during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transports nutrients/oxygen and removes metabolic waste products (e.g. via blood plasma) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lubricates joints and acts as a shock absorber (synovial fluid) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports digestion/absorption and metabolic reactions (solvent/medium) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how fluid replacement strategies can prevent a decline in performance in endurance events, with reference to the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Replacing fluid limits dehydration and associated reductions in endurance performance (e.g. earlier fatigue/increased perceived exertion) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains plasma/blood volume, supporting stroke volume/cardiac output and oxygen delivery to working muscles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports thermoregulation by allowing continued sweating and limiting excessive rises in core temperature (particularly relevant on Day 1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces cardiovascular drift and helps sustain pace/power output during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using an appropriate drink composition (water vs electrolyte-containing drink) can help maintain sodium balance and reduce risk of hyponatremia when large volumes are consumed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps maintain cognitive function/decision-making and reduces risk of cramping associated with fluid–electrolyte disturbance <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the roles of sodium and potassium in regulating hydration status.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium is the main extracellular cation; helps maintain extracellular osmolality and water retention <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium supports water absorption in the small intestine (sodium–glucose co-transport), improving fluid uptake during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium promotes thirst and helps conserve water via hormonal/kidney mechanisms (e.g. ADH/aldosterone-mediated reabsorption) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potassium is the main intracellular cation; helps maintain intracellular fluid volume/osmolality <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potassium is important for normal nerve impulse transmission and muscle contraction; imbalance can affect fluid distribution and function <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium and potassium gradients maintain osmotic balance across cell membranes and the distribution of water between compartments <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how hydration needs vary depending on environmental conditions and exercise intensity, with reference to the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- In hot/humid conditions (Day 1), sweat rate is higher, increasing total fluid requirements to limit dehydration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher exercise intensity increases metabolic heat production and ventilation, increasing fluid loss via sweat and respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater sweat losses increase electrolyte (especially sodium) losses; longer/hotter sessions may require electrolyte-containing fluids rather than only plain water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In cool conditions (Day 2), thirst can be blunted but fluid is still lost through respiration and sweat, increasing risk of under-drinking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individual variation (body size, fitness, sweat rate, acclimatization) means hydration strategies should be personalized <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pre-hydration status and planned intake opportunities influence needs during the session (access to fluids, pacing, duration) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over-drinking hypotonic fluid without replacing sodium can increase hyponatremia risk during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.4 Excess post-exercise oxygen consumption (EPOC) and recovery (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question directly asks for the best explanation of Excess Post-exercise Oxygen Consumption (EPOC) after interval training, which matches the leaf topic on EPOC and recovery.

options.content: EPOC occurs only after aerobic (steady-state) exercise, not after interval training.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: EPOC can occur after many exercise types, including interval training, especially when intensity is high.

options.content: EPOC helps repay the oxygen deficit and restore homeostasis.
options.markscheme: Correct: Post-exercise oxygen uptake remains elevated to support recovery processes (e.g., ATP-PC resynthesis, lactate oxidation, restoring O2 stores, thermoregulation) and return the body to homeostasis.

options.content: EPOC is solely the oxygen needed to convert all lactate into glycogen in the liver.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: Lactate can be oxidized and used as fuel and is not all converted to glycogen; EPOC is not solely explained by lactate clearance/gluconeogenesis.

options.content: EPOC is mainly due to increased oxygen delivery to muscles during exercise rather than after exercise.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: EPOC refers to elevated oxygen consumption after exercise has finished, not oxygen delivery during exercise.

questions.specification:
The table below outlines the physiological characteristics of different human energy systems.

| System | Primary Fuel Source | ATP Production Capacity | Typical Duration of Dominance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| X | Phosphocreatine | Low | 1–10 seconds |
| Y | Muscle Glycogen | Intermediate | 10–120 seconds |
| Z | Fatty Acids | High | >2 minutes |

Which of the following is an immediate physiological consequence during high-intensity exercise of the metabolic processes dominant in **System Y**?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: System Y uses muscle glycogen over ~10–120 s, indicating the glycolytic (anaerobic) energy system; a direct consequence is H+ accumulation and reduced muscle pH (lactate-associated acidosis), which is covered under phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative energy production.

options.content: A decrease in muscle $pH$ due to the accumulation of hydrogen ions
options.markscheme: System Y represents the glycolytic system (anaerobic glycolysis). This pathway breaks down glycogen without oxygen, resulting in the production of lactic acid which dissociates into lactate and hydrogen ions ($H^+$), subsequently lowering the $pH$ of the muscle tissue.

options.content: The primary utilization of the Krebs cycle within the mitochondria
options.markscheme: The Krebs cycle and the electron transport chain are components of the oxidative (aerobic) system, which corresponds to System Z in the table.

options.content: The breakdown of adipose tissue to release glycerol and fatty acids
options.markscheme: The mobilization of fats (lipolysis) is a slow process that provides fuel for the aerobic system (System Z) during prolonged, lower-intensity exercise.

options.content: An immediate increase in the storage of glycogen within the sarcoplasm
options.markscheme: During high-intensity exercise dominated by System Y, glycogen stores are depleted as they are consumed for ATP production, rather than increased.

questions.specification: Which factor would most likely increase an endurance athlete’s $VO_2\text{max}$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which factor would most likely increase an endurance athlete’s VO₂max; this directly targets determinants of VO₂max such as aerobic training, stroke volume, body composition, and lifestyle factors (e.g., smoking/inactivity).

options.content: Increased fat mass
options.markscheme: Increased fat mass increases non-functional body mass and does not improve the body's capacity for oxygen transport or utilization, often resulting in a lower relative $VO_2\text{max}$.

options.content: Consistent aerobic training
options.markscheme: Consistent aerobic training leads to physiological adaptations such as increased stroke volume, capillary density, and mitochondrial enzymes, which collectively increase $VO_2\text{max}$.

options.content: Reduced stroke volume
options.markscheme: A reduction in stroke volume would decrease the amount of oxygenated blood delivered to the muscles per heartbeat, thereby decreasing $VO_2\text{max}$.

options.content: Smoking and inactivity
options.markscheme: Both smoking (due to carbon monoxide binding with hemoglobin) and inactivity lead to a decrease in cardiovascular fitness and $VO_2\text{max}$.

An endurance runner performs a maximal effort for different durations during training. The table shows the estimated percentage contribution of the three energy systems during maximal exertion over various durations.

| Duration of maximal exercise | % ATP-CP system | % Anaerobic glycolysis (lactic acid system) | % Aerobic system |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 10 seconds | 75 | 20 | 5 |
| 30 seconds | 25 | 60 | 15 |
| 120 seconds | 5 | 40 | 55 |
| 1 hour | <1 | 1 | 98 |

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts 0 and (a) focus on ATP resynthesis via fatty acid oxidation during prolonged low-intensity exercise and the energy continuum/relative contributions of ATP-PC, glycolytic, and aerobic systems across durations.

parts.content: Describe how ATP is resynthesized during prolonged, low-intensity exercise using fatty acids.
parts.markscheme:
- Triglycerides are broken down (lipolysis) into glycerol and free fatty acids (FFAs) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- FFAs are transported into the mitochondria (via carnitine shuttle) to undergo beta-oxidation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Beta-oxidation removes two-carbon units from the fatty acid chain to form acetyl-CoA <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Acetyl-CoA enters the Krebs cycle; reduced coenzymes (NADH/FADH2) deliver hydrogen/electrons to the electron transport chain for oxidative phosphorylation to produce ATP <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 4 marks max. Do not accept answers focusing on anaerobic glycolysis or the ATP-CP system.</note>

parts.content: Analyse the concept of the energy continuum using the data in the table.
parts.markscheme:
- The energy continuum describes the overlapping contribution of all three systems; no system works in complete isolation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At 10 s the ATP-CP system is dominant (75%) due to the fastest rate of ATP resynthesis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- By 30 s anaerobic glycolysis becomes the largest contributor (60%) as phosphocreatine stores are depleted and glycolysis increases <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max.</note>

parts.content: Explain why the body transitions between energy systems as the duration of an event increases.
parts.markscheme:
- ATP-CP has high power but very low capacity; PC stores are limited so its contribution falls rapidly as duration increases <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Anaerobic glycolysis has moderate power/capacity but is limited by accumulation of hydrogen ions/lactate-associated fatigue, so it cannot dominate for long durations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The aerobic system has slower ATP resynthesis but much higher capacity; with sufficient oxygen delivery it becomes dominant (e.g., 55% at 120 s and ~98% at 1 h) and can use carbohydrates and fats <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max.</note>

parts.content: Outline three structural or functional differences between skeletal muscle tissue and smooth muscle tissue.
parts.markscheme:
- Appearance: skeletal muscle is striated, whereas smooth muscle is non-striated <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Control: skeletal muscle is voluntary (somatic), while smooth muscle is involuntary (autonomic) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shape/nuclei: skeletal fibres are long, cylindrical and multinucleated, whereas smooth muscle cells are spindle-shaped with a single central nucleus <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Location: skeletal muscle is attached to bones, whereas smooth muscle is found in the walls of hollow organs/blood vessels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contraction: skeletal muscle generally contracts faster and fatigues sooner; smooth muscle contracts more slowly and can maintain tone longer <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max. Accept any other valid differences.</note>

parts.content: Explain the difference between tidal volume and expiratory reserve volume (ERV) as an individual transitions from rest to exercise.
parts.markscheme:
- Tidal volume is the volume of air inspired or expired per normal breath and increases during exercise to meet higher oxygen demand <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Expiratory reserve volume is the additional volume of air that can be forcibly exhaled after a normal tidal expiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As exercise intensity rises and tidal volume increases, ERV decreases because a greater portion of the lung volumes is used within each breath (breathing more deeply into reserve capacities) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased tidal volume contributes to increased minute ventilation to maintain O2 uptake and CO2 removal during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max.</note>

questions.specification: Why is the oxidative system more efficient than the glycolytic system?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question compares oxidative vs glycolytic energy systems for a triathlete, focusing on aerobic ATP yield and lactate accumulation—core content of energy production via phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems.

options.content: It produces ATP at a faster rate than the glycolytic system.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The glycolytic system produces ATP at a faster rate than the oxidative system; oxidative is more efficient due to greater ATP yield per substrate and less fatiguing by-product accumulation.

options.content: It produces ATP aerobically without producing any by-products.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Although oxidative metabolism is aerobic, it still produces by-products (e.g. CO$_2$ and H$_2$O); it is considered more efficient mainly due to higher ATP yield and minimal lactate/H$^+$ accumulation compared with glycolysis.

options.content: It produces large amounts of ATP aerobically with minimal lactate accumulation.
options.markscheme: Correct. The oxidative system uses oxygen to produce a large ATP yield and, compared with the glycolytic system, results in minimal lactate/H$^+$ accumulation, supporting sustained endurance performance.

options.content: It only uses fat as a fuel source.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The oxidative system can use carbohydrates, fats, and (to a lesser extent) proteins; it is not limited to fat only.

parts.content: Describe how carbohydrates are used to provide energy during the first 300 m segment.
parts.markscheme:
- Carbohydrate is digested/broken down to glucose (or glucose is supplied from ingested carbohydrate) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glucose (and/or muscle glycogen-derived glucose) is used in glycolysis to produce ATP rapidly <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable detail:
- Anaerobic glycolysis predominates early at high intensity, producing lactate (consistent with 5.2 mmol L$^{-1}$ at 300 m) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of protein in recovery after resistance training.
parts.markscheme:
- Protein provides amino acids for repair of damaged muscle fibres/microtears <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stimulates muscle protein synthesis leading to hypertrophy/adaptation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps replace/maintain amino acid pool (positive nitrogen balance) following training <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable point:

parts.content: Outline the process and benefit of carbohydrate loading for athletes competing in endurance events.

- Typically taper/reduce training volume/intensity while increasing carbohydrate intake to maximise glycogen stores <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased muscle (and liver) glycogen increases carbohydrate availability during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Delays fatigue/maintains pace by sustaining blood glucose and reducing the need to rely on lower-intensity fat oxidation early on <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable point:
- Can improve endurance performance by allowing a higher sustainable intensity before glycogen depletion <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the energy system contributions during this 1500 m race simulation, using the split times and lactate values.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for a discussion linking energy systems to the stimulus data. For example:
- ATP-PC contributes most at the start/acceleration (very high power demand in early seconds), supporting the fast opening 0–300 m split (44 s) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Anaerobic glycolysis contributes substantially during high-intensity running, consistent with lactate already elevated at 300 m (5.2 mmol L$^{-1}$) and rising to 8.6 mmol L$^{-1}$ by 800 m <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aerobic system provides a large proportion of ATP across the middle of the race as duration increases, consistent with sustained high heart rates (e.g., 182–188 beats min$^{-1}$ by 800–1200 m) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the final segment, increased anaerobic contribution is indicated by the lactate rise to 12.4 mmol L$^{-1}$ and the ability to produce a fast closing split (49 s), while aerobic metabolism remains active but cannot meet total ATP demand alone <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable discussion points:
- Lactate accumulation/H$^+$ associated fatigue may contribute to pacing changes and the high end value at 1500 m <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Oxygen deficit at the start and increased reliance on anaerobic pathways early, with greater aerobic contribution as VO$_2$ rises <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how carbohydrates are utilized differently during aerobic and anaerobic exercise.

- in anaerobic exercise, carbohydrates are broken down without the presence of oxygen (anaerobic glycolysis) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Explain the contribution of the anaerobic glycolytic (lactic acid) system during a $400\text{ m}$ sprint.

- the anaerobic glycolytic system becomes the dominant energy provider after ATP-PC stores are depleted / after approximately $10\text{--}15\text{ seconds}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- this process results in the accumulation of lactic acid/lactate in the muscles and blood <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Outline how balanced macronutrient intake supports sports performance.
parts.markscheme:

- proteins support muscle repair, growth, and recovery after exercise <marks>A1</marks>
- fats serve as a fuel source for low-intensity or long-duration activity <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the interplay of energy systems during a $1500\text{ m}$ middle-distance race.
parts.markscheme:
- all three energy systems (ATP-PC, anaerobic glycolytic, and aerobic) contribute simultaneously, shifting in their relative contribution (energy continuum) <marks>A1</marks>
- the ATP-PC system initiates the race, providing immediate energy for the first $10\text{--}15\text{ seconds}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- the anaerobic glycolytic (lactic acid) system supports sustained speed in the early stages and during any mid-race surges <marks>A1</marks>
- the aerobic system becomes the dominant provider for pacing as the race progresses and oxygen delivery meets demand <marks>A1</marks>
- toward the end of the race, the anaerobic glycolytic system contribution increases to support a final sprint finish <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
The following table outlines the characteristics of different metabolic pathways used during physical activity:

| Pathway | Main Fuel Source | Primary By-products | Rate of ATP Production |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **W** | Phosphocreatine | Inorganic phosphate | Instantaneous |
| **X** | Glycogen | Lactic acid and H+ ions | Fast |
| **Y** | Fatty acids | Carbon dioxide and water | Slow |
| **Z** | Amino acids | Nitrogenous waste | Very slow |

Which pathway is the dominant contributor to energy production for a cyclist completing a $90$-minute steady-state ride at $60\%$ of their maximal heart rate?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.1 Energy production through phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which metabolic pathway (phosphagen, glycolytic/anaerobic, oxidative/aerobic—fat vs carbs vs protein) predominates during a 90-minute steady-state ride at ~60% HRmax, which is energy systems selection based on intensity and duration.

options.content: Pathway W
options.markscheme: Pathway W represents the phosphagen system (ATP-CP), which is exhausted within the first $10$ seconds of high-intensity activity and would not be the dominant system for a $90$-minute ride.

options.content: Pathway X
options.markscheme: Pathway X represents anaerobic glycolysis. While it provides energy quickly, its reliance on glycogen and the production of lactic acid makes it unsuitable as the primary energy source for long-duration, steady-state aerobic exercise.

options.content: Pathway Y
options.markscheme: Pathway Y represents the oxidative (aerobic) system using fatty acids. This system has a high capacity and is the primary energy source for low-to-moderate intensity activities lasting longer than $20$-$30$ minutes, such as a $90$-minute steady-state cycle.

options.content: Pathway Z

questions.specification:
The table below displays the physiological characteristics of the three main energy systems in the human body.

| Energy System | Maximal Rate of ATP Production ($mol \cdot min^{-1}$) | Total Energy Capacity ($mol$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Phosphagen | 3.6 | 0.7 |
| Glycolytic | 1.6 | 1.2 |
| Oxidative | 1.0 | 90.0 |

Based on the data provided, which factor most limits how long the phosphagen system can remain the dominant ATP supplier before another energy system becomes dominant?

options.content: A slow maximal rate of ATP resynthesis
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the phosphagen system has the highest maximal rate of ATP production in the table, so rate is not the limiting factor for duration.

options.content: The rapid accumulation of lactic acid as a byproduct
options.markscheme: Incorrect: rapid lactic acid accumulation is associated with the glycolytic system, not the phosphagen system.

options.content: A very limited total capacity for energy production
options.markscheme: Correct: the phosphagen system has the lowest total energy capacity (0.7 mol), which limits how long it can remain dominant.

options.content: The high requirement for oxygen during the reaction

questions.specification:
The following table displays data collected during a maximal oxygen consumption ($VO_2max$) test for two endurance athletes. Convert absolute $VO_2max$ from $L\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ to $\mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ (1 $L$ = 1000 $ml$) to compare relative $VO_2max$.

| Athlete | Body mass ($kg$) | Absolute $VO_2max$ ($L/min$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Athlete X | $75$ | $4.5$ |
| Athlete Y | $60$ | $4.2$ |

Which statement correctly compares the aerobic fitness of these two athletes based on their relative $VO_2max$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.3 Energy systems > A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3.2 Factors affecting maximal oxygen consumption (VO₂ max)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question requires converting absolute VO₂max (L/min) to relative VO₂max (ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹) using body mass and then comparing aerobic fitness, which directly targets understanding/usage of VO₂max and factors affecting it (e.g., body mass).

options.content: Athlete Y has a higher relative aerobic capacity ($70\ \mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$) than Athlete X ($60\ \mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$).
options.markscheme: Correct. Relative $VO_2max$ = $\frac{\text{absolute }VO_2max\ (ml\ \mathrm{min}^{-1})}{\text{mass }(kg)}$. Athlete X: $4.5\ L\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}=4500\ ml\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$; $4500/75=60\ \mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$. Athlete Y: $4.2\ L\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}=4200\ ml\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$; $4200/60=70\ \mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$.

options.content: Athlete X is more aerobically fit because their absolute oxygen uptake is $0.3\ L/min$ higher than Athlete Y.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Aerobic fitness comparison here is based on relative $VO_2max$ ($\mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$), not absolute $VO_2max$; Athlete Y’s relative value is higher.

options.content: Athlete Y has a lower relative aerobic capacity because they weigh less and consume less total oxygen per minute.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Athlete Y consumes slightly less total oxygen per minute (4.2 vs 4.5 $L\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$), but due to lower body mass their relative $VO_2max$ is higher, not lower.

options.content: Both athletes have the same relative $VO_2max$ because the ratio of mass to oxygen uptake is identical.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The ratios are different: $4500/75=60$ and $4200/60=70\ \mathrm{ml\ kg}^{-1}\ \mathrm{min}^{-1}$, so relative $VO_2max$ is not the same.
